food_subgroup: Rice-based salad with fermented tea leaves

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry exists for Burmese tealeaf rice salad. FOODON_00002809 (rice salad food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Rice dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Burmese laphet thoke htamin thoke.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, representing a single-plate portion as typically served at a Burmese or Myanmar-cuisine restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: cooked white rice (USDA FoodData Central #168878), fermented tea leaves and condiments estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data and USDA SR Legacy mixed-dish analogy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / FAO INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for tealeaf rice salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model: approximately 60% cooked white rice (USDA #168878), 15% fermented tea leaves (estimated from green tea leaf composition, FAO/INFOODS SEA), 10% fried peanuts (USDA #16087), 5% dried shrimp (USDA #15152), and 10% mixed condiments (fish sauce, lime juice, garlic, sesame, chilli). Values represent a plausible mid-range estimate and should be verified against laboratory analysis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed rice-based dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked white rice GI (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tealeaf rice salad. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant ingredient being cooked white rice (GI ~64–72 plain), adjusted downward to approximately 54 due to the presence of fermented tea leaves, peanuts, sesame, lime juice, and protein/fat components which are known to lower the glycemic response of mixed rice dishes. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed rice salads and rice dishes with added fat, protein, and acid. GI category is low (≤55). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (54 × 24.5 × 3) / 100 = 39.7, rounded to 39.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Tealeaf rice salad contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 960 mg per standard 300 g serving), primarily from fish sauce and dried shrimp; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from white rice, partially offset by fat, protein, and fermented tea leaf components
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 54 and a glycemic load of approximately 39.6 per 300 g serving, tealeaf rice salad presents a moderate glycemic burden for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control to 150–200 g and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables is advised to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile from peanuts and sesame; moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile of tealeaf rice salad is predominantly unsaturated (peanuts, sesame seeds), which is cardioprotective; however, the sodium content from fish sauce warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and portion moderation is recommended to limit overall sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tealeaf Rice Salad (လက်ဖက်ထမင်းသုပ်, Laphet Thoke Htamin Thoke) is a Burmese-origin dish that combines cooked white rice with fermented tea leaves (laphet), fried garlic, roasted peanuts, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, fish sauce, lime juice, fresh tomato, and chilli. The dish is assembled by tossing all components together, resulting in a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously earthy, tangy, savoury, nutty, and mildly spicy. The fermented tea leaves provide a distinctive bitter-umami depth that is characteristic of Burmese cuisine. Texturally, the dish contrasts soft rice with crunchy fried garlic, peanuts, and sesame. In Singapore, tealeaf rice salad is served at Burmese and Myanmar-cuisine restaurants as a main course or sharing dish. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy mixed dish (~165 kcal/100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution from dried shrimp and peanuts, predominantly unsaturated fats, and a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~54) due to the fat, protein, and acid components moderating the rice starch digestion rate. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The fermented tea leaves contribute polyphenols and antioxidants. This dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains peanuts, sesame, shellfish, and fish allergens.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for tealeaf rice salad exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA FoodData Central). All nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model using USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA analogues. GI is an analogous estimate based on mixed rice dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is low pending laboratory analysis or an approved composite dish entry.
